data.title: White House touts Don Lemon arrest
data.description:
The White House is standing firmly behind its decision on Friday to arrest former CNN anchor Don Lemon. 

“When life gives you lemons…” the White House account posted on the social platform X on Friday with a chains emoji. The text accompanied a photo of Lemon during his recent coverage of a protest inside a St. Paul, Minn., church. 

Lemon said he was at the protest in the capacity of a journalist.  

The Trump administration has sought to detain eight people, including Lemon, for their role at the protest against the federal immigration crackdown in the state. Demonstrators believed the church’s pastor was an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer. 

The Justice Department says protesters violated the FACE Act — a law that protects people from attacks in places of worship. 

Trump responded to the protest in a Truth Social post, saying the protesters were “professionals.”

“They are highly trained to scream, rant, and rave, like lunatics, in a certain manner, just like they are doing,” Trump said. “They are troublemakers who should be thrown in jail, or thrown out of the Country.”

White House Deputy Chief of Staff James Blair noted in an X post on Friday that a federal jury had indicted Lemon prior to his detainment, saying, “He was not just magically arrested.” 

The Thursday grand jury indictment follows a decision by a Minnesota magistrate judge last week to reject the federal charges against Lemon related to the Minnesota church protest. 

Federal officials detained Lemon while he was covering the Grammy Awards in Los Angeles on Thursday evening, his lawyer said in a statement. 

Lowell said Lemon will challenge the charges “vigorously and thoroughly in court.” 

“The First Amendment exists to protect journalists whose role it is to shine light on the truth and hold those in power accountable,” Lowell said.

data.title: RFK Jr. on adding Trump's name to Kennedy Center: 'I've got bigger fish to fry'
data.description:
Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. is speaking out about President Trump’s name being added to the Kennedy Center, saying there are “more important” things than “a name on a building.”

“I was here when the Center was built,” the Health and Human Services secretary told ITK on Thursday, when asked about the Kennedy Center’s board voting last month to rename it “The Donald J. Trump and the John F. Kennedy Memorial Center for the Performing Arts” to honor the current and 35th presidents.

“The people who were involved in making this happen, they wanted to build it because of President [John F.] Kennedy’s commitment to… projecting American culture around the world — it was, my uncle believed, our great export,” the late president’s nephew said of the intention behind the creation of the performing arts institution, built in 1971 as a living tribute to Kennedy.

“President Trump feels the same way,” said the 72-year-old son of former Attorney General and Sen. Robert F. Kennedy (D-N.Y.) when ITK caught up with him at the premiere of “Melania,” held at the Kennedy Center.

Praising the commander in chief, Kennedy said, “He’s put tremendous energy thinking about this center, making sure that the building is beautiful.”

Trump, in an unprecedented move last year, overhauled the Kennedy Center’s board. After accusing it of being too “woke,” he replaced board members and named himself its chairman. He’s since toured the arts hub several times, vowing to beautify the space.

“We’re going to fix it up, but it’s really emblematic of our country,” Trump said in March. 

While the Center’s interim President Richard Grenell said that the “decision of the Trump Kennedy Center Board doesn’t impact the Memorial to Kennedy set up by Congress,” several members of the Kennedy family harshly condemned the move.

Kerry Kennedy, president of the Robert & Ethel Kennedy Human Rights Center and the younger sister of Robert F. Kennedy Jr., said in December, “President Kennedy proudly stood for justice, peace, equality, dignity, diversity, and compassion for those who suffer.”

“President Trump stands in opposition to these values, and his name should not be placed alongside President Kennedy’s,” she said. 

Maria Shriver, the daughter of former President Kennedy’s sister, Eunice Kennedy Shriver, expressed “disbelief” at the time after hearing of the name change. 

But Robert F. Kennedy Jr. indicated his focus was elsewhere.

“If I can save one kid’s life, it’s more important than a name on a building,” he said. 

“For me, I’ve got bigger fish to fry.”

data.title: Congress has the chance to help save the US shrimping industry
data.description:
In 2000, Texas was home to more than 2,500 licensed commercial shrimpers. Today, it is home to less than 1,000. According to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Fisheries for the Gulf of America, there are only 1,467 federally permitted vessels in the entire Gulf of America.

Domestic shrimp contributed to approximately 50 percent of U.S. consumption in the 1980s. Now, imported, foreign shrimp accounts for more than 90 percent of the shrimp consumed in the United States, with domestic shrimp accounting for just 4 percent -10 percent of consumption.

You may be wondering what happened to the nation’s once prosperous shrimping industry. In 1980, the United States imported approximately 250 million pounds of shrimp. In 2024, the U.S. imported around 1.67 billion pounds of shrimp, with most of it coming from India, Ecuador and Indonesia. It is widely known that shrimp imported into our country are not wild-caught — they are shrimp grown on farms or hatcheries that use child labor and are ridden with dangerous working conditions.

Since the 1970s, multilateral institutions such as the World Bank and the United Nations have provided hundreds of millions of dollars in support of foreign shrimp operations in Asia and Latin America, including Ecuador, India and Belize, with Ecuador receiving the most financial aid.

In fact, between 1980 through 2000, the World Bank contributed over $950 million in loans to support shrimp aquaculture in Ecuador. The United States has executive directors representing our country and domestic industries at these multilateral institutions and is the most significant funder of these organizations. U.S. law requires U.S. executive directors at international financial institutions to vote against funding any project to produce or extract any commodity for export that would cause harm to U.S. producers of the same commodity or if there is a global surplus of that commodity.

Unfortunately, U.S. executive directors at these international financial institutions have historically disregarded this statute, which is intended to protect domestic producers like America’s shrimpers.

However, in April 2025, for the first time in history, the U.S. voted against funding a shrimp aquaculture project at an international financial institution.

While I appreciate the U.S. Treasury Department finally standing up for American shrimpers by voting against an Asian Development Bank project to fund $150 million in Thai shrimp production, this “no” vote was long overdue. For far too long, American tax dollars have been bank rolling foreign shrimp production, to the demise of our own shrimping industry.

Enough is enough.

For the last two congresses, I have proudly introduced the Save Our Shrimpers Act, legislation which would prohibit federal funds from being made available to international financial institutions to finance any activity relating to shrimp farming, shrimp processing, or the export of shrimp from any foreign country to the U.S. My legislation would also trigger a Government Accountability Office investigation and require annual reports to Congress outlining to what extent U.S. representatives at international financial institutions comply with existing law.

We need to completely turn off the money tap that has been using hard-working American tax dollars to bankroll foreign shrimp production while America’s shrimpers are struggling to stay afloat.

It is imperative that Congress passes my bill, the Save Our Shrimpers Act, expeditiously so that American shrimpers have the chance to survive. We need to codify my bill into permanent law, so that American shrimpers are protected, regardless of who occupies the White House.

I’m calling on Congress to do the right thing and to move my America First legislation so that American shrimpers can continue to do what they love for generations to come.

Troy Nehls represents Texas’s 22nd District in the U.S. House of Representatives.

data.title: Independent journalist Georgia Fort records moments before arrest over Minnesota church disruption
data.description:
“Agents are at my door,” independent journalist Georgia Fort told followers during a live stream on Facebook early Friday morning.

The founder of BLCK Press, a self-described social enterprise newsroom in Minnesota, was filming the moments before she surrendered to federal authorities on unspecified charges related to a disruption during a St. Paul church service earlier this month.

“You guys, I wanted to alert the public that agents are at my door right now,” Fort said at the start of the two-and-a-half-minute live stream.

Fort said that she was advised by her attorneys to “go with them…down to Whipple,” referencing the federal building in Hennepin County where protests against the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown have grown in recent weeks.  

Someone could be heard crying in the background as Fort noted her children were inside the home.

“This is all stemming from the fact that I filmed a protest as a member of the media. We are supposed to have our constitutional right of freedom to film, to be a member of the press. I don’t feel like I have First Amendment right as a member of the press because now federal agents are at my door, arresting me for filming the church protest a few weeks ago,” Fort said.

Fort was taken into custody alongside three others, including former CNN anchor Don Lemon, in connection with what Attorney General Pam Bondi described as a “coordinated attack on Cities Church in St. Paul, Minnesota.”

It remains unclear what specific charges the individuals could face, though Justice Department officials had previously indicated possible prosecutions under the FACE Act, a law that protects access to houses of worship “free of force or threats of force.”

The arrests come weeks after demonstrators entered a church where one of the pastors was believed to be affiliated with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), interrupting a service with chants of “ICE out!”

Several independent journalists, including Fort and Lemon, were on the premises and documented the incident, which drew the ire of top Trump administration officials.

“You’re not allowed to stop people from worshiping, and you’re not allowed to intimidate or impede them. And so, the individuals that went in there and did that — and you can tell that they had a plan,” Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche said during a Fox News appearance.

“You can tell they had an agenda. You cannot do that in this country, and we’re investigating it, and I expect we will move very aggressively and very quickly to find the people that did this,” he added.

Both Fort and Lemon have argued they were there not as participants but to provide news coverage.

Fort told viewers on Friday that she knew she was on a “defendant’s list” and had been following the advice of her attorneys, reiterating that she felt her constitutional rights were being infringed upon.

She then walked to the front door, noting that agents were “knocking,” as another person is heard saying: “she’s coming right now.”

The video cuts off as Fort reaches to turn the doorknob.

data.title: Klobuchar says Trump making US 'less safe'
data.description:
Sen. Amy Klobuchar (D-Minn.) said President Trump is endangering American citizens amid his administration’s immigration crackdown in the state. 

Klobuchar, who jumped into the Minnesota governor’s race on Thursday, told MS NOW’s Lawrence O’Donnell that voters want to live in “places that are safe.”

“[Trump] is making them less safe,” she told the host. “The three first fatalities in the city of Minneapolis — three so far this year — two have been committed by federal agents.”

Immigration enforcement officials shot and killed resident Renee Good and Veterans Affairs ICU nurse Alex Pretti in separate instances in Minneapolis earlier this month. Their deaths have caused Democrats, and some Republicans, to question the Department of Homeland Security’s (DHS) use of force in carrying out the president’s deportation agenda.

Trump administration officials have defended the federal agents and maintained that the officers acted in self-defense. The incidents have also ramped up scrutiny of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and many lawmakers have called for the DHS chief to be held accountable.

Klobuchar on Thursday noted that she spoke to Pretti’s parents after he was killed last weekend, who said they wanted his “story told.”

“They described him as the kind-hearted man that he was, but they also said the hardest thing for them 24 hours later was what the officials in the administration, the Trump administration,” the Minnesota Democrat added.

On the day Pretti was killed, White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller called the 37-year-old protester a “domestic terrorist” and “would-be assassin” in separate posts on the social platform X. 

The president doubled down on his criticism of Pretti on Friday after a video circulated earlier this week appearing to show the late protester involved in a separate clash with federal agents prior to his death. He also rebuffed claims from border czar Tom Homan suggesting the administration would relax immigration operations in Minneapolis.

Klobuchar also expounded on her decision to jump into the North Star State’s gubernatorial race to replace outgoing Gov. Tim Walz (D). Walz announced earlier this month that he would not seek reelection amid a probe into alleged fraud involving the state’s social services programs.

The senator said the decision to run felt like something she “had to do” amid tensions in her home state. 

“I love my job, but I love my state more,” Klobuchar added. “And our state has been the center of America’s heartbreak, but we’ve also been the center of America’s hope and courage.”

data.title: DOJ releases final tranche of Epstein documents
data.description:
The Justice Department said Friday they have completed their review of the Epstein files, releasing 3 million additional documents to the public.

Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche told reporters Friday that the tranche being released will be one of the last steps required under a bill passed by Congress and signed into law by President Trump requiring the publication of the files related to deceased sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

“Today’s release marks the end of a very comprehensive documentation – document identification and review process to ensure transparency to the American people and compliance with the act. The department has engaged in an unprecedented and extensive effort to do so, after submitting the final report to Congress, as required under the Act, and publishing the written justifications for redactions in the Federal Register, the department’s obligations under the under the act will be will be completed,” Blanche said.

The deputy attorney general said he did not expect the release to quell interest in the case or end speculation about its contents but added that the Justice Department has not refused to charge any one who may have had sexual relations with or trafficked victims.

“There’s a hunger or a thirst for information that I do not think will be satisfied by the review of these documents and…there’s nothing I can do about that,” Blanche said.

“There’s this built in assumption that somehow there’s this hidden tranche of information of men that we know about, that we’re covering up, or that we’re choosing not to prosecute. That is not the case. I don’t know whether there are men out there that abuse these women. If we learn about information and evidence that allows us to prosecute them, you better believe we will,” he added.

“But I don’t think that the public or you all are going to uncover men within the Epstein files that abuse women, unfortunately.”

data.title: No, antitrust law does not prohibit NCAA eligibility rules
data.description:
Good for Charlie Baker. Amid an upheaval in college athletics, the National Collegiate Athletic Association’s president has been taking a stand for the organization’s ability to define the boundaries of who is eligible to play college sports. 

The last few years have seen the NCAA become somewhat reluctant to test the limits of its power to police college athlete eligibility — once a classic NCAA function. That is due, in part, to the prevailing belief that enforcing eligibility rules would violate antitrust law.

But that belief is incorrect.

In a new statement, Baker made clear that athletes who have signed contracts with National Basketball Association teams are not eligible to return to NCAA competition. The fact that the NCAA president even needed to make this statement shows how much the times have changed. 

Several antitrust lawsuits have moved forward against the NCAA. In the absence of comprehensive legislation from Congress on collegiate athletics, the NCAA might believe itself to be operating in a sort of legal twilight zone.

Student athletes have pushed the envelope. In 2024, Vanderbilt quarterback Diego Pavia brought an antitrust suit against the NCAA to obtain an extra year of eligibility. The NCAA, applying its longstanding rules, had ruled that Pavia’s junior college years counted toward his college eligibility and precluded him from playing another season. But a federal district court judge granted Pavia a preliminary injunction that allowed him to play in 2025. 

More recently, reports indicated that Chicago Bulls rookie Trentyn Flowers — who has already signed an NBA contract — would pursue NCAA eligibility.

But the NCAA is pushing back. And Baker’s statement on eligibility for players who have signed NBA contracts is a step in the right direction. The power to define who can and cannot play in a collegiate sporting event is integral to the NCAA’s ability to administer college athletics. 

True, the Supreme Court held in NCAA v. Alston that NCAA restrictions on educational benefits for student-athletes violated antitrust law. But that does not mean that the NCAA is prohibited from maintaining any eligibility rules. Rather, the question in an antitrust case brought under the Sherman Act (as challenges to NCAA regulations often are) is whether a given restraint of trade is reasonable. And, in general, eligibility restrictions in sports are reasonable.

Sports have always occupied a special place in antitrust law. As Judge Robert Bork explained in his famous book, “The Antitrust Paradox,” league sports are “perhaps the leading example” of activities that “can only be carried out jointly.” 

Although antitrust law enforces competition, “competition” is itself the product in sports. Thus, courts typically grant latitude to sports leagues and governing bodies to regulate competition. The closer to the on-field product, the less of an antitrust problem — for example, no one would question the NCAA’s ability to specify the overtime rules in football.

The NCAA has strong arguments that the Sherman Act is altogether inapplicable to the eligibility restraints that have been in the news. 

Whereas the rules at issue in Alston were tied directly to student-athlete compensation, these eligibility rules relate instead to the status of the athlete — whether he has been in college for a certain number of years or whether he has signed a pro contract. Any impact on the player’s ability to obtain compensation for his publicity rights is merely incidental. To be sure, a federal appeals court declined to adopt this theory as a rule in a recent decision about NCAA eligibility rules.

But even if the Sherman Act applies, courts should uphold these status-based eligibility rules as reasonable restraints of trade in service of maintaining the integrity of the NCAA’s unique product. To ensure that the NCAA continues to offer “college” sports, it must draw the line somewhere. Tying eligibility to enrollment is an obvious manifestation of the principle. And, following that line of logic, eligibility cannot be unlimited. 

The whole point is that the opportunity to play college sports is a fleeting moment in a young man or young woman’s life, associated with that student’s pursuit of an undergraduate degree (with narrow exceptions). When a student chooses to take the leap to the professional ranks in a sport and signs a professional contract, the NCAA has made a reasonable determination that his or her eligibility to continue playing that sport in college must come to an end. 

This rule ensures a clean delineation between college athletics and professional sports, establishing a meaningful distinction between the two products (as antitrust law allows) and protecting the college game.

The NCAA understands this, and the organization needs some flexibility to bring order to college sports. Here’s hoping, for the sake of the game, that they get it.

Eli Nachmany is an attorney in Washington, where he practices antitrust and sports law.

data.title: CNN blasts 'unacceptable' arrest of former host Don Lemon
data.description:
CNN is blasting the Trump administration over the arrest of Don Lemon, a former host at the network who was detained after covering anti-Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) protesters who entered a church in Minnesota to demonstrate earlier this month.

Lemon’s arrest came after a judge rejected federal charges against the journalist and critic of President Trump in connection with his attendance at the protest. Prosecutors have not unsealed an indictment as of Friday morning.

“The FBl’s arrest of our former CNN colleague Don Lemon raises profoundly concerning questions about press freedom and the First Amendment,” the news network said in a statement. “The Department of Justice already failed twice to get an arrest warrant for Don and several other journalists in Minnesota, where a chief judge of the Minnesota Federal District Court found there was ’no evidence’ that there was any criminal behavior involved in their work.”

Lemon’s attorney has argued he was acting as a journalist covering the protest and should be protected by the First Amendment.

CNN echoed that sentiment in its statement defending the host it fired in 2023.

“The First Amendment in the United States protects journalists who bear witness to news and events as they unfold, ensuring they can report freely in the public interest, and the DOJ’s attempts to violate those rights is unacceptable,” the network said. “We will be following this case closely.”

After leaving CNN, Lemon started an independent online show and has been sharply critical of the president and his policies.

A number of press freedom groups and allies of Lemon have blasted his arrest and pointed to it as an example of the Justice Department’s desire to crack down on perceived enemies of the president.

The White House cheered the arrest on social media, posting a photo along with the text “When life gives you lemons…”

data.title: Crockett on Lemon arrest: 'Are you f—ing kidding me?!'
data.description:
Rep. Jasmine Crockett (D-Tex.) did not mince words when reacting to the arrest of former CNN anchor Don Lemon by federal authorities on Friday.

“Are you F-ing KIDDING ME?! @Donlemonbsky has been arrested by the DOJ?! Exercising your first amendment constitutionally ‘protected’ right gets you locked up, exercising your 2nd gets you killed, and actual murder… well in uniform, gets you nothing!” Crockett wrote on Friday morning on the social platform X.

“This IS NOT NORMAL nor OK!” she added.

Lemon was taken into federal custody alongside three others in connection with “the coordinated attack on Cities Church in St. Paul, Minnesota,” according to a statement from Attorney General Pam Bondi.

His attorney, Abbe Lowell, said Lemon was detained in Los Angeles, where he was covering the Grammy Awards. An indictment and specific charges have not yet been unsealed.

The other individuals arrested were Trahern Jeen Crews, Georgia Fort, and Jamael Lydell Lundy, Bondi said, promising “more details soon.”

Lemon had been put “on notice” by Justice Department officials earlier this month for interrupting a Minnesota church service, as the state became embroiled in protests over the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement operations and the fatal shooting of 37-year-old Renee Good by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer.  

“A house of worship is not a public forum for your protest! It is a space protected from exactly such acts by federal criminal and civil laws! Nor does the First Amendment protect your pseudo journalism of disrupting a prayer service,” Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon, the head of the Justice Department’s Civil Rights Division, wrote on the social platform X on Jan. 18.

Lemon argued he was there not as a participant but as a journalist covering the demonstrations.

On the premises, Lemon filmed and interviewed an activist who was part of a group of demonstrators that entered the church, where one of the pastors also works for ICE. He also interviewed the head pastor of the church, who reportedly asked him to leave.

The Justice Department sought to bring charges in connection with the incident under a law that protects access to houses of worship “free of force or threats of force.”

A magistrate judge rejected that bid last Thursday, a ruling Lowell told the Associated Press affirmed that his work was protected by the First Amendment.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries also slammed Lemon’s arrest, saying there was “zero basis” for the decision and that he should be “freed immediately.”

data.title: Federal judge blocks death penalty for Luigi Mangione
data.description:
A federal judge dismissed some of Luigi Mangione’s charges on Friday for allegedly gunning down United Healthcare CEO Brian Thompson in 2024, preventing prosecutors from seeking the death penalty.

Mangione will still stand trial on his two remaining charges, which accuse him of causing Thompson’s death while stalking him. It could land him in prison for life. 

The Justice Department’s (DOJ) now-defunct bid to execute him relied upon declaring those stalking allegations a “crime of violence.” 

U.S. District Judge Margaret Garnett ruled it doesn’t meet the legal definition. She noted the “apparent absurdity” of her conclusion and criticized Supreme Court precedent she said is “totally divorced” from actual conduct in criminal cases. 

“However, regardless of its own views, a district court is duty-bound to follow binding Supreme Court precedent,” Garnett wrote. 

The judge is an appointee of former President Biden who serves in New York City. 

“The analysis contained in the balance of this Opinion may strike the average person — and indeed many lawyers and judges — as tortured and strange, and the result may seem contrary to our intuitions about the criminal law,” she wrote. 

“But it represents the Court’s committed effort to faithfully apply the dictates of the Supreme Court to the charges in this case,” Garnett continued. “The law must be the Court’s only concern.” 

The opinion landed just ahead of a scheduled hearing in Mangione’s case Friday morning. 

His legal team did not have any immediate response. The Hill has reached out to DOJ for comment. 

Mangione stands accused of shooting and killing Thompson outside a Midtown Manhattan hotel in December 2024. The case quickly garnered national attention as a multi-day manhunt for the suspect ended with police arresting the suspect at a McDonald’s in Pennsylvania. 

He will now proceed to trial in the federal case on two counts that accuse him of causing Thompson’s death while stalking him.

In addition to the federal indictment, Mangione faces state charges in both Pennsylvania and New York, but he does not face the prospect of the death penalty in those cases. 

Mangione has pleaded not guilty to all of the charges.

Earlier this week, a Minnesota man was arrested for allegedly trying to break out the suspect out of the Brooklyn facility where he is being detained.
